bottles.name: Dragonette Seven
bottles.notes:
Grape: Syrah
Region: Santa Barbara, California
Drink Now or Cellar? Drink now — drinking beautifully, don't wait too long
Price Point: $$
Pairs With: Lamb, red meats, hearty dishes

I didn't anticipate that our first bottle experience on KazmiCellars.com would be one where I wasn't actually drinking wine. But here we are.
It's the night before Mother's Day. I'm fresh off a hip replacement, on enough medication to sedate a large animal, and Rachel has been an absolute rock through all of it — taking care of me without complaint, without hesitation. The least I could do was open something special for her.
Dragonette is our oldest standing wine membership we still hold from Santa Barbara. It means something to us. So the 2018 Syrah was an easy choice — it's one of Rachel's favorites and honestly I don't think we could have done better.
This isn't your traditional big, brooding Syrah. Think medium-plus bodied, elegant more than powerful. We noticed plum, a little blueberry, a subtle spice that weaves through without ever taking over. Nothing heavy, nothing aggressive. Just a beautifully composed wine that's had just enough time in the bottle to find itself. The 2018 vintage was a perfect call.
It paired beautifully with lamb shank. Let it breathe for about 45 minutes to an hour beforehand. Trust me on that. The wine opens up and the pairing becomes something else entirely.
I did manage to steal one sip. It made me extremely jealous of Rachel. Worth every drop.


## Who Should Grab This

If you love Syrah but find the big Napa style reds a little too much, Dragonette is your answer. Approachable, refined, and the kind of bottle you open when the moment deserves it.

## The Kazmi Take

**A membership we'll never give up. The 2018 is drinking beautifully right now. Don't wait too long on it. Happy Mother's Day, babe. 🍷**

bottles.name: Luigi Tecce Poliphemo
bottles.notes:
Grape: Aglianico
Region: Campania, Southern Italy
Drink Now or Cellar? Either — drinking well now, will only improve with 2 to 3 more years
Price Point: $$
Pairs With: Steak, rich meats, hearty Italian dishes

What do you do four or five days before major surgery? If you're me, you call your closest friends and head to your favorite restaurant. No fanfare, no big announcement. Just good people, a great table, and the understanding that a night like this deserves a wine worth remembering.
I arrived at La Stella Italian Steakhouse and Music Lounge in Addison about thirty minutes early and found my way to the bar. I asked for Garrett, my favorite sommelier, a guy who has never steered me wrong. I told him exactly what I was looking for. A few weeks back I had an incredible Rhône blend from Sardinia that stopped me cold and I couldn't for the life of me remember what it was. I said find me something I wouldn't typically order. A grape I wouldn't reach for on my own. A region I haven't explored. He didn't hesitate.
He came back with the 2018 Luigi Tecce Poliphemo from Campania, Italy. An Aglianico. Now the first time I ever had Aglianico was at Kenneth Volk in Santa Barbara, and I fell in love with the grape then. But a Campanian Aglianico? I had never gone there. Garrett told me there was only one catch, it needed an hour, maybe two, to open up properly. No problem. He opened it, decanted it, and kept me company with a little Burgundy in the meantime.
By the time our group sat down, ordered, laughed, and settled into the kind of conversation that only happens with people who really know you, the Poliphemo was doing its thing. Opening up slowly, like a bouquet coming to life in the glass. When Garrett finally poured it at the table it stopped everyone mid sentence. My crew is used to Silver Oak, Caymus, maybe a Prisoner or two. Good wines, great wines even. But when you're with me I don't cherry pick the familiar. I go for the discovery. And Garrett delivered.
Smoke. Leather. Texture. Tannins that were soft but absolutely lingering. It had weight without being heavy, complexity without being complicated. Paired with steak it was nothing short of perfect. Aglianico was made for a hearty dish and this one proved it beyond any doubt.


## Who Should Grab This: 

Anyone who thinks they've figured out Italian reds and wants to be proven beautifully wrong. If you love big structured reds but want something that tells a story most people at the table have never heard, this is your bottle.


## The Kazmi Take

** Garrett, you were on point. This one goes straight onto the wish list and one day it's coming home to the Kazmi Cellar. Don't sleep on Campania, this Aglianico will give Barolo a run for its money. As last suppers go, this one was worthy. 🍷**

bottles.name: 2018 Charles Vienot Fixin 1er Cru Clos du Chapitre
bottles.notes:
Grape: Pinot Noir
Region: Fixin, Côte de Nuits, Burgundy
Drink Now or Cellar? Drinking beautifully right now
Price Point: $$ — exceptional value for a Premier Cru
Pairs With: Duck, salmon, mushroom dishes, light red meat

Before we get into the bottle let me tell you something. The first time I ever encountered Fixin was at a restaurant called Monarch right here in Dallas. I saw it on the wine list, pointed to it, and called it exactly what it looks like. Fixin. Like a Texan about to get something done. The sommelier leaned over politely and quietly corrected me. It is pronounced Fisson. The X is silent. You are welcome.
We found this particular bottle in a small wine shop in a little commune tucked between Gevrey-Chambertin and Marsannay during our 2024 Burgundy trip. One of those moments where you are just wandering a wine shop and somethting recognizable shows up; particularly in Burgundy we were no experts; Into the cart it went, we shipped back half a case!
Back in Dallas, we opened it on the patio. The first thing you notice is the freshness. It is a 2018 so it is not young but for a Burgundy not too old either; it greeted us with a brightness that was unexpected. Not overly floral, something more subtle and restrained. We gave it a good swirl and walked away for thirty minutes. When we came back it had transformed to a bit more of what I expected. Red cherry, a little raspberry, all those beautiful Côte de Nuits red fruits slowly making their entrance exactly as they should. We nursed it over a full hour and it rewarded us every single pour.
But here is the best part. As I sat there sipping this wine and looking over at Rachel, I realized the bottle was doing something extraordinary. It was transporting us. Back to the winding roads between Burgundy villages, back to the wine shops and the tastings and the moments we collected together on that trip. Being able to bring all of that back to our patio in Dallas with one bottle is something no price tag can capture.


## Who Should Grab This
If you love Burgundy but feel priced out of the big names like Gevrey-Chambertin or Chambolle-Musigny, Fixin is your answer. This is a genuine Côte de Nuits Premier Cru at a fraction of the price of its famous neighbors. For anyone building a cellar on a budget or just wanting to explore what Burgundy really tastes like without breaking the bank, this commune is a must. Just remember, it is Fisson. Not Fixin. 🍷


## The Kazmi Take

*Underrated, elegant, and full of memory. The Fixin commune is one of our go-to's in Burgundy and this Charles Vienot did not let us down. Find it, buy more than one, and open it somewhere that deserves it. 🍷*

bottles.name: Domaine Barville Châteauneuf-du-Pape, Brotte
bottles.notes:
Grape: Grenache dominant blend
Region: Châteauneuf-du-Pape, Southern Rhône, France
Drink Now or Cellar? Cellar 2 to 3 more years for best results, drinking well now
Price Point: $$ — exceptional value for the appellation
Pairs With: Lamb, beef, rich stews, hearty dishes

We had already taken our journey down Burgundy that evening with the Fixin. The bottle was empty, the memories were full, and the night still had more to give. So why not keep traveling. We crossed the invisible line from Burgundy south to the Rhône Valley and opened this.

We had the pleasure of visiting the Brotte vineyard in Châteauneuf-du-Pape during our 2024 France trip. Walking those grounds, tasting in that appellation, understanding the land the wine comes from — that changes how you drink it back home. This bottle came back with us and we have been waiting for the right moment.

I opened it about an hour and a half ahead of time, decanted it and it needed every minute of it. At first touch it was fruit forward, a little young, not quite ready. For me that was expected that's why I let it breathe. By the time we sat down with it the transformation thatbI hopednfor became real. A little pepper coming through from the Syrah, beautiful cherry notes from the Grenache, everything sitting together in a well balanced, satisfying glass of southern France.
Here is my honest take on where this wine is going. Another two to three years and this becomes almost perfect. Is it a fifteen to twenty year bottle? I am not sure. I kept a couple in the cellar to find out and I will report back.
What I can tell you right now is that at fifty euros a bottle, even factoring in the cost of shipping it back from France (pre tarriffs), this is one of the best value plays in our entire cellar. Châteauneuf-du-Pape at that price point with this kind of quality is not something you walk past.

## Who Should Grab This
Anyone who loves big Southern Rhône reds and wants a genuine Châteauneuf-du-Pape without paying the premium of the most famous names in the appellation. This is a cellar bottle, not a drink tonight bottle, so if you have the patience give it a couple of years. If you are ever in the area visit the Brotte vineyard and bring some home yourself. The experience of drinking a wine from a place you have actually stood makes every sip taste different.

## The Kazmi Take
*Bang for your buck does not begin to cover it. Fifty euros shipped back from Châteauneuf-du-Pape for a wine this well crafted is a steal. It is in our cellar and it should be in yours. 🍷*

bottles.name: Kuleto Estate Malbec
bottles.notes:
Grape: Malbec
Region: Napa Valley, California
Drink Now or Cellar? Drinking beautifully now, will hold another 2 to 3 years
Price Point: $$
Pairs With: Birria tacos, braised meats, anything spiced or grilled
Best For: Malbec lovers who have only explored Argentina and want to see what Napa does with the grape

Some evenings you do not plan, they just come together. Rachel's close friend was joining us for a family dinner and we had already decided on birria tacos. Rich, spiced, deeply satisfying. I thought about what to open and the answer came quickly. A Malbec. A rich, aromatic red that could hold its own against bold flavors without fighting them. The 2018 Kuleto Estate Malbec from Napa Valley had been waiting for exactly this kind of night.
Kuleto Estate farms high elevation vineyards above Lake Hennessey in Napa Valley, which gives their wines a freshness and complexity you do not always find on the valley floor. This Malbec is a perfect example of what that elevation can do.
On the nose, violet and black fruit come forward immediately. That floral lift is Malbec's signature and Kuleto delivers it beautifully. Give it a good swirl and the palate opens up, dark plum, more black fruit, a touch of leather weaving through, everything sitting together in a wine that is very well balanced with good acidity and alcohol right around fourteen percent. Nothing overdone. Nothing missing.
The pairing with birria tacos was exactly what I hoped for. The richness of the wine matched the richness of the meat, the acidity cut through the fat, and the spice of the tacos made the dark fruit in the glass pop even more. Perfect harmony.
But the best part of the evening had nothing to do with the wine itself. Rachel's friend had never heard of Kuleto. She took one sip, looked at the bottle, and started taking photos of the label. That moment right there, introducing someone to a wine they had never encountered and watching it connect, that is what this is all about. We were building memories around a table with good food, great wine, and people we love.
We opened another Kuleto after that. A different bottle, a different story. That one is coming soon.

## Who Should Grab This
If you love Malbec but have only ever explored Argentina, Napa Valley Malbec is a revelation worth having. Richer, more structured, with that signature floral violet lift that makes the grape so immediately appealing. Kuleto Estate is a producer worth knowing. This bottle pairs beautifully with anything spiced, braised, or grilled. Birria tacos included.

## The Kazmi Take
*A perfect pairing, a new discovery for a friend at our table, and a second bottle opened before the night was done. The 2018 Kuleto Estate Malbec delivered on every level. Find it, open it with people you love, and let the violet do the rest. 🍷*

bottles.name: Kuleto Estate
bottles.notes:
Grape: Zinfandel
Region: Napa Valley, California
Drink Now or Cellar? Drinking beautifully now, benefits from decanting 45 minutes minimum
Price Point: $$
Pairs With: Braised meats, BBQ, spiced dishes, or honestly just good conversation
Best For: Anyone who wants a bold, expressive red that demands you slow down and enjoy the evening

The Malbec had been so good that stepping it up felt like the only natural thing to do. Rachel's friend was still at the table, the lingering warmth of birria tacos still in the air, and the evening had that quality where nobody wanted it to end. So we popped open the 2018 Kuleto Estate Zinfandel. And then came the moment.
The first nose stopped me. Powerful, bold, unapologetic. Jammy blackberry, a little leather, dried herbs, and an alcohol presence that told me immediately this wine needed time. I decanted it right away and we waited forty five minutes. That is not a suggestion with a Zinfandel like this. That is a requirement.
When we came back the second nose was a completely different conversation. The boldness had softened into something more inviting. The alcohol had quieted down to a faint presence rather than a shout. We poured it into the glass and just looked at it for a moment. Deep purple, gorgeous, the kind of color that makes you feel like you are about to drink something significant. The viscosity was perfect and those legs were hanging on the glass for dear life.
First taste. An explosion. There is really no other word for it. Jammy dark fruit, black pepper spice, a richness that filled the entire palate. We do not typically drink Zinfandel without food but the lingering of the Malbec and the tacos was still there, and this wine stood on its own beautifully.
We decanted half of it upfront and left the other half in the bottle, decanting the rest about halfway through the evening. The wine opened up slowly and kept revealing itself with each pour. A little spice kept things interesting. The conversation deepened. Nobody looked at their phone.
That is what Zinfandel does when it is done right. It is not a wine you drink fast. It is slow, like a nice  late spring evening on the patio where you are not counting the hours, you are just living inside one.
Rachel's friend reached into her purse for her camera again. Second bottle of the night. Second photo. We have a Kuleto fan among us now.

## Who Should Grab This
Anyone who loves bold expressive reds and has the patience to let them breathe. This is not a pour and go wine. Give it time, open up the conversation, and let the evening unfold around it. Perfect for a dinner party, a backyard gathering, or any night that deserves to go long.

## The Kazmi Take
*The Malbec opened the door. The Zinfandel pulled everyone deeper into the room. Two bottles, one evening, one new Kuleto fan created at our table. The 2018 is everything a Napa Zinfandel should be — bold, generous, and in absolutely no hurry. 🍷*

bottles.name: Dragonette Duvarita Pinot & 2018 Domaine d'Ardhuy Savigny-lès-Beaune Premier Cru Les Peuillets
bottles.region: Santa Barbara and Cote de Beaune
bottles.notes:
Side by Side — 2018 Dragonette Duvarita Vineyard Pinot Noir vs 2018 Domaine d'Ardhuy Savigny-lès-Beaune Premier Cru Les Peuillets
Quick Take
Grape: Pinot Noir — both, same vintage, completely different worlds
Dragonette: Bright, cherry, fresh, fruit forward, 14% alcohol
d'Ardhuy: Mineral, earthy, plum, old world elegance, 13.5% alcohol
Drink Now or Cellar? Both drinking beautifully now
Price Point: $$ each
Pairs With: Duck, salmon, mushroom dishes, charcuterie
Best For: Anyone who wants to understand what climate does to a grape
Two bottles that both mean something to Rachel and I. Dragonette is a long standing relationship, a membership we have held for years, a winery that has never let us down. The d'Ardhuy came home with us from Burgundy, picked up during a trip that changed how we think about Pinot Noir forever. Drinking a Burgundy in our own house next to a Dragonette, my two personal favorite regions for this grape in the same evening, was a moment I had been looking forward to for a while.
I chose 2018 for both. Same vintage, same grape, completely different worlds. I figured both would be ready to go and I was right. No food this time. Just Rachel, me, and two glasses of Pinot Noir. A proper geek out.
The First Nose
I opened both and poured them at the same time. No waiting, just immediate first impressions side by side.
The Dragonette came out fresh and bright. Cherry right away, a little alcohol on the nose, floral lift that is classic for their style. The d'Ardhuy was an entirely different conversation. Mineral, earthy, plum but with the skin on it, a little mushroom quality underneath. Sitting there with both glasses in front of me it almost felt like two different grapes.
After 45 Minutes
I set them both down and came back. After a good swirl and a proper second nose the Dragonette had muted slightly, settling into itself. The d'Ardhuy leaned further into its minerality, becoming even more distinctly old world. Both still smelled fantastic. Just further apart from each other than when we started.
In the Glass
The visual difference told the same story. The Dragonette showed a classic pale ruby garnet, good legs on the glass but nothing overly viscous as you would expect from a well made Pinot. The d'Ardhuy sat somewhere between red and black plum in color, still translucent but slightly deeper than the Dragonette. Both exactly where they should be.
Alcohol told a story too. I noted 14% on the Dragonette which is on the higher end for Pinot Noir but becoming more and more common in California. I prefer a little less but I do not mind it when the wine is balanced. The d'Ardhuy came in at 13.5%, and that half percent difference is noticeable. It shows in how the wine sits on the palate.
On the Palate
The Dragonette delivered exactly what the nose promised. Cherry, brightness, a little warmth from the alcohol but well integrated. The d'Ardhuy was mineral and structured, that plum with skin quality carrying through from the nose, earthy and complex in a way that made you slow down and pay attention.
Both drank beautifully. Rachel had similar thoughts throughout. We geek out together on evenings like this and she is usually right. We ended the night leaning into the Dragonette, its brightness winning the late rounds. But the d'Ardhuy was the more thought provoking wine of the two. The one that made you sit quietly for a moment before reaching for your glass again.

## Who Should Grab This
If you want to understand what climate does to a grape, this is your experiment. Open a California Pinot and a Burgundy from the same vintage on the same night and just pay attention. You do not need to be an expert. The difference will find you. Dragonette Duvarita is available through their membership and occasionally at select retailers. Domaine d'Ardhuy is findable at good wine shops and worth hunting down.

## The Kazmi Take
*Two bottles. Two regions. One grape. One incredible evening. The Dragonette reminded us why we have been loyal to this winery for years. The d'Ardhuy reminded us why Burgundy never really lets you go. Do this experiment at home. You will not regret it. 🍷*

bottles.name: 2015 La Sala "Il Torriano" Chianti Classico Gran Selezione
bottles.notes:
Grape: Sangiovese
Region: Chianti Classico, Tuscany
Drink Now or Cellar: Both, drinking beautifully now, will age 15 to 20 more years easily
Price Point: Under €100 at the winery, great value for Gran Selezione
Pairs With: Bistecca Fiorentina, lamb, aged pecorino, wild boar ragu
Best For: The wine lover who wants serious Tuscany without paying Brunello prices

We were in Tuscany in 2019, moving through the region from Montalcino to Chianti Classico, and La Sala was one of our favorite stops. Rachel and I spent the afternoon walking their vineyards with people who genuinely understood their land. They showed us the soil, the rocks, walked us through an organic winemaking process that felt more like a philosophy than a method. We tasted through their lineup, tried their dessert wines, and then the 2015 Gran Selezione landed in front of us. That was the year our daughter was born. We bought a case on the spot.
We opened one this past weekend. The moment it hit the glass you got it immediately, that earth, that classic Sangiovese translucence, real viscosity in the pour. We let it breathe. Thirty minutes in it started opening up. At an hour it was perfect. Still, patient, confident. That's the thing about wines like this, they're in no rush. They wait for you.
Gran Selezione is the top tier of Chianti Classico, stricter selection, longer aging requirements, best vineyards. And 2015 was one of the greatest Tuscany vintages in a generation. This bottle could sit for thirty more years. But it doesn't need to. It's singing right now.

## Who Should Grab This
Anyone who wants to understand what Chianti Classico is really capable of without paying Brunello prices. This is serious, age worthy Sangiovese that punches well above its price point. If you've ever been to Tuscany, or dreamed about going, this bottle will take you there.

## The Kazmi Take
*Every bottle in our cellar is a memory with a cork in it. This one takes us straight back to walking those Tuscan hills, talking terroir with people who loved what they made. The fact that it's also the vintage of our daughter's birth makes it something else entirely. We'll be opening these for years. Pick some up. You won't regret it.*

bottles.name: Casa Madero 3V Gran Reserva & Monte Xanic Gran Ricardo
bottles.region: Valle de Parras, Coahuila & Valle de Guadalupe, Baja California
bottles.notes:
Mexico on the Table — A World Cup Wine Feature
The World Cup is here and we are raising a glass to the host nations. Starting with Mexico and two bottles that tell the full story of what Mexican wine is capable of.
Casa Madero 3V Gran Reserva 2018 — Valle de Parras, Coahuila
Casa Madero was founded in 1597. That is not a typo. The oldest winery in the Americas, predating most of the wine world's most celebrated estates by centuries. The Spanish colonial land grant text printed on the label is not decoration. It is history. Valle de Parras sits in the high altitude desert of northern Mexico where dramatic temperature swings between day and night preserve acidity and build structure. The 3V is a Cabernet Sauvignon, Merlot, and Shiraz blend, aged in oak, polished and serious. This is a wine that carries 400 years of winemaking behind it and shows every bit of it.
Monte Xanic Gran Ricardo 2020 — Valle de Guadalupe, Baja California
This is the other side of the Mexican wine story. Valle de Guadalupe sits about an hour south of San Diego, a coastal valley with a Mediterranean climate that shares DNA with the best of California wine country. Monte Xanic is one of the founding estates of the region and Gran Ricardo is their flagship, named after founding partner Ricardo Aguirre. Cabernet Sauvignon dominant with fruit from both Valle de Guadalupe and the cooler Ojos Negros subzone. Structured, elegant, and a clear statement that Baja California belongs in the conversation with the world's great wine regions.
Two bottles. Two regions. One country that has been making wine longer than almost anywhere else in the New World. Mexico deserves your attention.

## The Kazmi Take
Most people don't think about Mexico when they think about wine. These two bottles are exactly why they should. Casa Madero represents heritage that most wine regions can only dream about. Monte Xanic represents a modern wine scene that is evolving rapidly and producing world class bottles. The World Cup brought us back here. The wine is what will keep us coming back.

bottles.name: Ultreia Godello
bottles.notes:
Grape: Godello
Region: Bierzo, Northwest Spain
Drink Now or Cellar: Drink now, built for freshness
Price Point: $$
Pairs With: Mussels, octopus, grilled seafood, lighter pasta
Best For: White wine lovers wanting something beyond Chardonnay and Sauvignon Blanc

We were sitting at the bar at Barcelona Wine Bar in Dallas and the first pour of the night was this, the Ultreia Godello 2023 from Bierzo by Raul Perez. We had mussels and octopus in front of us and honestly could not have picked a better match if we tried.
The wine is bright, immediately alive in the glass. Light stone fruit, a touch of citrus, and that mineral quality that Godello is known for, clean and precise with a slightly saline edge on the finish. It's the kind of white that works completely on its own but really comes alive when food is involved. The mussels pulled out the salinity. The octopus matched the texture. Everything clicked.
Raul Perez is one of the most celebrated winemakers in all of Spain and Bierzo is his home territory. He works with old vine Godello on the granite and slate soils of northwest Spain and what he produces here is a textbook example of why this grape is having a moment.

## Who Should Grab This
Anyone who has been drinking Chardonnay and Sauvignon Blanc and wants to try something with more personality. Godello is crisp and approachable but has real depth underneath. This is a great entry point into Spanish white wine and one of the best value bottles on any menu it appears on.
## The Kazmi Take
We ordered this not knowing what to expect and it set the tone for the entire evening. Bright, mineral, food friendly, and completely unpretentious. Raul Perez made something special here and at this price point there is no reason not to have a few of these in your cellar.

bottles.name: Com Tu Garnacha
bottles.notes:
Grape: Garnacha, 100%
Region: Montsant, Catalonia, Spain
Drink Now or Cellar: Drink now, opens beautifully right away
Price Point: $$
Pairs With: Pork croquettes, charcuterie, roasted meats, Spanish tapas
Best For: Old World red lovers wanting Priorat character at half the price

The second bottle of the night was the Com Tu from Clos Mogador, 100% Garnacha from Montsant, and it arrived just as the pork croquettes landed on the bar. That pairing was not planned. It was perfect.
Com Tu opened up right away, no waiting, no coaxing. The Garnacha came through immediately, bright red cherry, fruit forward but grounded in that earthy Old World character that separates Spanish Garnacha from its New World counterparts. There is something wild and honest about it. Lively on the palate, soft tannins, medium body, and a finish that keeps the earthiness going longer than you expect.
Montsant is the appellation that surrounds Priorat on three sides. It shares similar rocky, mineral terroir at a fraction of the price. Clos Mogador is one of the legends of the region, and Com Tu is their expression of pure Garnacha at its most approachable and joyful.

## Who Should Grab This
Anyone curious about Priorat but not ready to spend Priorat prices. Montsant gives you the same rocky terroir, the same Old World Garnacha character, and the same sense of place at a much more accessible price point.
## The Kazmi Take
This bottle is fun. Not every wine has to be serious and Com Tu knows exactly what it is. A glass of pure, honest Garnacha that makes whatever food is in front of you taste better. The label with the illustrated face says it all. This wine has personality.

bottles.name: Coster dels Olivers, Garnacha
bottles.notes:
We closed the evening with the Coster dels Olivers 2021 from Priorat and it arrived just as the parrilla dishes came out. The timing was right. The pairing was right. Everything about the final act of that night was right.
Priorat takes its time. That's just the nature of the appellation. The Coster dels Olivers was no different, it needed a few minutes to open up, but it did so without a decanter, which tells you something about how well this particular vintage is drinking. Once it came around the wine revealed a darker, more sophisticated profile than the Com Tu. Deeper fruit, more structured, the llicorella slate soils of Priorat coming through in that characteristic mineral graphite quality that defines the region.
This is not a wine that shouts. It's a wine that commands. By the time it opened fully it was one of those bottles where the conversation stops and everyone at the bar just looks at their glass.
Priorat is one of only two DOCq appellations in all of Spain alongside Rioja. That designation means something. Old vines, slate soils, limited yields, serious intent. The Coster dels Olivers carries all of that with elegance rather than aggression.

## Who Should Grab This
Anyone who appreciates sophistication in a glass. Priorat is not a beginner wine but it is not inaccessible either. Give it time, pair it with something serious off the grill, and let it show you what it can do.
## The Kazmi Take
The perfect ending to a perfect Spanish wine evening. Priorat always reminds us why certain appellations earn their reputation. This one lived up to every bit of it. The parrilla was excellent. The wine was better.

bottles.name: Trimbach Gewurztraminer
bottles.notes:
Grape: Gewurztraminer
Region: Alsace, France
Drink Now or Cellar: Both. 2018 is drinking beautifully now and will only improve with more time.
Price Point: $$
Pairs With: Spiced dishes, Asian cuisine, foie gras, soft washed-rind cheeses, charcuterie
Best For: Anyone who thinks they only like dry whites. This wine changes minds.

We opened this before dinner. Our guests had just arrived from Zurich and we wanted to ease into the evening with something that felt like a welcome, not a statement. The 2018 Trimbach Gewurztraminer was exactly that.
There is a reason this is our favorite Gewurztraminer. Trimbach has been making wine in Ribeauvillé in Alsace since 1626, one of the oldest wine families in France, and they approach Gewurztraminer with a restraint that most producers do not bother with. Where other Alsatian Gewurztraminers lean into the sweetness and pile on the perfume, Trimbach keeps it focused. Dry, precise, aromatic without being overwhelming. This is Gewurztraminer at its most serious.
The 2018 vintage was drinking perfectly. Lychee, rose petal, fresh ginger, a hint of honey, and a long dry finish that kept pulling you back for another sip. The color in the glass was a deep golden yellow, beautiful to look at before you even brought it to your nose.
Our friend from Zurich typically gravitates toward drier whites, the kind of person who raises an eyebrow at anything with even a whisper of sweetness. She loved it. That is the Trimbach effect. The aromatics draw you in and the dry, structured finish earns your respect. It does not taste like what people expect Gewurztraminer to taste like. It tastes like what Gewurztraminer should taste like.
We still have a few bottles left in the cellar and after that evening we made a decision to leave them alone a little longer. That is how good this was. The wine told us to wait.
## Who Should Try This
Anyone who has ever dismissed Gewurztraminer as too sweet or too perfumed. Trimbach is the antidote to that assumption. Also the perfect bottle for guests who want something elegant and different before a serious meal. It sets the table beautifully.
## The Kazmi Take
The right bottle for the right moment. Our Swiss guests brought their discerning European palates to our table and Trimbach held its own completely. We opened it as a welcome and it became the conversation starter of the evening. The 2018 still has years ahead of it. If you find a few bottles, buy them and be patient. They will reward you.

bottles.name: 2014 Justin Isosceles
bottles.notes:
Grape: 73% Cabernet Sauvignon, 15% Cabernet Franc, 12% Merlot, unfiltered
Region: Paso Robles, California
Drink Now or Cellar: Drinking beautifully now, will hold comfortably through 2030
Price Point: $$
Pairs With: Osso bucco, braised short ribs, lamb, any rich slow-cooked meat
Best For: Bordeaux lovers who want New World boldness with Old World structure and age-worthiness

Some bottles find the right moment without any planning. This was one of them.
Rachel's friends were visiting from Zurich and she had been in the kitchen all day working on her osso bucco. The kind of meal that earns its own bottle. We went into the cellar and pulled the 2014 Justin Isosceles. At ten years old this wine was ready in every sense of the word.
Isosceles has been Justin Winery's flagship wine since its first release in 1987, quickly gaining critical acclaim for its structure, balance, and age-worthiness. The name reflects the geometric precision and balance strived for in every vintage.  (Ripley Wines) Justin founded his winery in Paso Robles in 1981 when the region was still largely unknown, long before anyone was calling it one of California's great wine destinations. He bet on Bordeaux varieties in a warm climate and won.
The 2014 is a blend of 73% Cabernet Sauvignon, 15% Cabernet Franc, and 12% Merlot, unfiltered, and it comes in at 16% alcohol, which tells you everything about how ripe and concentrated this vintage was in Paso Robles. Wine Enthusiast called it a whopper of a wine, noting barely ripe black cherry, green peppercorn, and charred fennel on the nose, with a palate loaded with both savory and ripe flavors, from roasted black plums to oregano and dill, very complex and chalky in texture.  (Wikipedia)
What we experienced at the table was exactly that. The wine opened up over the course of the evening, getting better with every pour. Dark fruit, baking spice, a hint of cedar, and that signature Paso Robles warmth and richness. It shows a measure of finesse and restraint given its size, big and expressive, with notes of plum, blackberry, baking spices, and cedary oak.  (Galiena Pte. Ltd)
Against Rachel's osso bucco it was a revelation. The richness of the braised veal, the brightness of the gremolata, the deep tomato-based braise, all of it met the wine exactly where it was. Our guests from Zurich were not expecting this from a California bottle. That is the best compliment Isosceles could receive.
## Who Should Grab This
Anyone who loves Bordeaux but wants to explore what Paso Robles does with the same varieties. Isosceles is built on the same philosophy, Cabernet Sauvignon as the backbone with Cab Franc and Merlot adding complexity and softness, but it speaks in a distinctly Californian voice. Warmer, richer, more immediate.
## The Kazmi Take
The right wine at the right table on the right night. Rachel's osso bucco deserved a serious bottle and the Isosceles delivered in every way. Our guests flew in from Zurich, had world class wine at their doorstep back home, and still asked about this one before they left. That is what Justin Isosceles does. It impresses people who know what impressive looks like

bottles.name: Feudi di San Gregorio Greco di Tufo
bottles.notes:
Grape: Greco, 100%
Region: Greco di Tufo DOCG, Irpinia, Campania, Italy
Drink Now or Cellar: Drinking beautifully now, will develop over the next 5 years
Price Point: $$
Pairs With: Grilled fish, shellfish, calamari, roast chicken, or on its own
Best For: White wine lovers who want structure and minerality, and a warm night on the patio
Feudi di San Gregorio Greco di Tufo 2021 — Irpinia, Campania

Some wines just belong to a moment. This one belonged to a warm summer night in Dallas, out on the patio, and it could not have been more perfect.
We opened the 2021 Feudi di San Gregorio Greco di Tufo, a wine we keep in the cellar for exactly these kinds of evenings. Feudi di San Gregorio is the benchmark producer of Campania, the winery that did more than anyone to put southern Italy's native grapes back on the world stage. Their Greco di Tufo is a modern classic, made from 100% Greco grown in the volcanic, tuff-rich soils around the town of Tufo in Irpinia.
In the glass it poured a clear, pale gold. The nose was clean and precise, medium in intensity, with mineral, pear, and peach coming through. On the palate it delivered exactly what it promised, flint, stone fruit, and that distinctive volcanic minerality that makes Greco di Tufo so special. The acidity was medium plus, bright and lifting, with a medium finish that kept the whole thing balanced and fresh. This is a very good to excellent wine, structured and textured in a way that most Italian whites simply are not. It has real presence, closer to a white Burgundy in weight than the light, easy whites people expect from Italy.
But the best part was the setting. Well chilled, sipped slowly on its own as the Dallas heat finally softened into evening, it was pure pleasure. No food needed, no occasion required, just a great glass of wine and a warm night. That flinty minerality and bright acidity make it endlessly refreshing, the kind of white that makes you slow down and enjoy where you are.

** Who Should Grab This
Anyone who loves white wine with structure and minerality rather than just fruit. If your idea of Italian white is a simple Pinot Grigio, Greco di Tufo will open your eyes. It is also one of the few Italian whites with genuine aging potential, so buy a few and watch it develop. And if you have a patio and a warm evening, you already have the perfect occasion.

** The Kazmi Take
This is exactly why we keep bottles like this in the cellar. Clear pale gold, mineral and stone fruit on the nose, flint and peach on the palate, medium plus acidity holding it all together. Very good to excellent, and absolutely singing well chilled on its own on a warm Dallas night. Feudi di San Gregorio makes the benchmark, and this bottle showed exactly why. Pour it cold, find a patio, and let the evening do the rest.

bottles.name: Kay Brothers Mery Block 6 Shiraz
bottles.notes:
Grape: Shiraz, 100%, from vines planted in 1892
Region: McLaren Vale, South Australia
Drink Now or Cellar: Drinking well now with a decant, will hold and improve through 2040
Price Point: $$$
Pairs With: Wild boar, grilled red meat, game, aged hard cheeses
Best For: Shiraz lovers who want power with finesse, and anyone who thinks they know what old-vine Australian Shiraz tastes like

Some wines just need a minute. This one needed a decant, and it was worth every second of the wait.
I found the Kay Brothers Block 6 Shiraz on the list at Barrel, a wine bar in San Mateo, and could not pass it up. This is one of Australia's most storied Shirazes, made from a single block of vines planted in 1892 on the historic Amery vineyard in McLaren Vale. The 2017 vintage marked 125 years since that block went into the ground. That is not a number you see very often on a wine label.
Poured young, it came out an inky, almost purple color, dense and dark in the glass. It needed air to come around, so we gave it time, and the transformation was exactly what you hope for. Hints of black pepper and cooked dark berries emerged, deep and brooding, with that savory old-vine character underneath.
What surprised me most was the texture. For a wine with this reputation for power and structure, the tannins were softer than I expected. Silky, even. And at 14.5% alcohol you would think the heat would show, but I never felt it. Not once. That is the mark of a wine in balance, where everything is so well integrated that the alcohol simply disappears into the whole. Iron fist, velvet glove, exactly as they say.
We paired it with wild boar and it was a beautiful match. The richness and gaminess of the boar met the dark fruit and pepper of the Shiraz head on, each one lifting the other. But here is the thing. As good as it was with the food, the wine finished amazing entirely on its own. Once the plates were cleared, it kept opening, kept evolving, and those last pours on their own were the best of the night.
This is a wine that rewards patience twice over, once in the decanter and once in the glass.
## Who Should Grab This
Anyone who loves Shiraz and wants to taste what happens when the vines are over a century old and the winemaking is done with real restraint. If your idea of Australian Shiraz is a jammy fruit bomb, Block 6 will change your mind. It has power, yes, but it wears it with grace.
## The Kazmi Take
I picked the Kay Brothers over a couple of other serious bottles and had zero regrets. Inky purple, black pepper, cooked dark berries, tannins softer than expected, and not a hint of heat despite the alcohol. It sang with the wild boar and then somehow got even better on its own after dinner. Give it a decant, give it time, and let it show you why this wine has been made for over 125 years

bottles.name: Patz & Hall Dutton Ranch Chardonnay
bottles.notes:
Grape: Chardonnay
Region: Russian River Valley, Sonoma County, California
Drink Now or Cellar: Drink now, at its freshest over the next 2 to 3 years
Price Point: $$
Pairs With: Roast chicken, creamy pastas, soft cheeses, seafood, or on its own
Best For: Chardonnay lovers who want balance over bombast, and anyone stuck in an airport who wants to drink well
*Patz & Hall Dutton Ranch Chardonnay — Russian River Valley
Airport wine is usually a compromise. This was not.
Sitting in the American Airlines lounge in Dallas, I spotted Patz & Hall Dutton Ranch Chardonnay available by the glass and jumped on it. The bartender had just opened the bottle and even fresh off the cork it was drinking beautifully.
Patz & Hall built their reputation on single-vineyard Chardonnay and Pinot Noir from some of California's best sites, and Dutton Ranch in the Russian River Valley is one of the most respected names in Sonoma. This bottling is a benchmark for what moderate-climate California Chardonnay should be.
In the glass it was exactly what you hope for from this style. Tropical and stone fruit notes up front, medium acidity, medium body, and a really well-balanced whole. Nothing overdone, no heavy-handed oak or butter bomb here, just a clean, complete, harmonious Chardonnay that reflects where it comes from. The Russian River fog keeps the fruit fresh and the acidity intact, and you can taste that balance in every sip.
It is a reminder that the right producer makes good wine anywhere you find it, even standing at a lounge bar between flights.
## Who Should Grab This
Anyone who loves balanced California Chardonnay and has written off the category as too oaky or too fat. Patz & Hall is the corrective. And if you happen to see it by the glass at an airport lounge, you have just upgraded your travel day considerably.
## The Kazmi Take
Finding a genuinely good glass of wine at the airport feels like a small win, and this Patz & Hall Dutton Ranch delivered. Tropical and stone fruit, medium body, medium acidity, beautifully balanced. Classic moderate-climate Sonoma Chardonnay done right. Sometimes the best pours find you when you least expect them, even in a terminal in Dallas.
